title: Building a laid paper mould
subtitle: Stretching fine brass wire across a wooden frame to make the traditional sheet-forming tool
excerpt: A laid paper mould, a wooden frame spanned with closely spaced parallel wires and a second set of wires running at right angles, leaves the characteristic laid and chain-line watermark pattern in every sheet formed on it. The mould can be made at home from a simple wooden frame and fine brass wire. The result is the same structure that European papermakers have used since the fourteenth century.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943), Hunter gives detailed descriptions of laid mould construction drawn from his first-hand study of historic European moulds and his own papermaking practice. The construction method he describes is unchanged from the pre-1928 tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: The closely spaced parallel marks visible when a sheet of traditional laid paper is held up to light. They are the impression left by the fine wire wires running across the mould from side to side.

The more widely spaced lines running at right angles to the laid lines in traditional laid paper. They mark the heavier wires that hold the laid wires in place, running lengthways along the mould at intervals of roughly 25 to 30 mm.

The removable wooden frame that sits over the mould during sheet formation to contain the pulp and define the sheet's edges. Removed after forming; the slightly ragged edge it produces is the deckle edge.

content.text: are the fingerprints of traditional European papermaking. The mould that makes them is simple in principle: a rigid frame spanned with fine wires at 1 mm intervals. Making one is a patient piece of work rather than a technically complex one. The skill is in keeping the laid wires evenly tensioned.

items.name: Laid mould wire, fine brass wire, 0.2 to 0.3 mm diameter
items.substitutions: Fine stainless steel jewellery wire (same gauge); do not use copper or galvanised wire, which corrodes in the wet papermaking vat

items.name: Wooden frame, interior A5 (148 × 210 mm), planed hardwood
items.qty: one mould frame, one deckle frame
items.substitutions: A clean picture frame with the glass removed; resin-sealed MDF for short-run work

content.text: Sand the mould frame smooth and seal the wood with two coats of varnish or shellac. An unsealed frame will swell and warp in the papermaking vat, distorting the wire surface. Allow the sealing coat to dry completely before proceeding.
Mark a series of points along the two short sides of the frame at 1 mm intervals using a fine pencil and ruler. Use the bookbinding awl to make shallow starter holes at each point.

Begin at one end and run the laid wire back and forth across the frame, passing through each starter hole and pulling taut at each turn. The wire should be under consistent tension, not so tight that it cuts into the wood, but with no visible sag.

When all laid wires are in place, add the chain wires, thicker lengths of wire running lengthways at 25 mm intervals, woven over and under the laid wires and fixed at each end. The chain wires hold the laid wires in position and add structural support to the surface.

The deckle is a second frame of exactly the same outer dimensions as the mould. It sits on top of the mould during sheet formation, held down by both hands, and contains the pulp slurry while the water drains through the wire. A well-fitting deckle produces a clean deckle edge; a loose one allows pulp to seep under the frame and produces a ragged edge.

items.symptom: Wires sag in the middle of the frame
items.cause: Insufficient tension during stringing, or the wire gauge is too fine for the frame width.
items.fix: Re-string, pulling each wire tighter before knotting. For frames wider than 200 mm, use 0.3 mm wire rather than 0.2 mm.

items.symptom: The wire surface corrodes after a few uses
items.cause: Pulp water is too acidic, attacking the brass wire.
items.fix: Test the vat water pH and adjust to neutral (pH 7). See the pH testing entry for papermaking.
